title: Hegseth's Testosterone Policy Was Mocked Across Four Languages
subtitle: China stayed silent. The UK's medical experts said the science doesn't support it. Everyone else found it absurd.
body_md:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth announced mandatory annual testosterone screening for all active-duty U.S. service members aged 30 and over, with testosterone replacement therapy available through the Pentagon for those who test low. The announcement generated mockery across four languages and near-universal skepticism from establishment outlets, with one notable exception: China's state media did not cover the story at all.

[AFP/France 24](https://www.france24.com/en/us-defense-secretary-pete-hegseth-announces-testosterone-screening-for-us-troops) reported the policy in neutral terms; [France 24's French service](https://www.france24.com/fr/am%C3%A9riques/20260716-homme-high-t-nouvelle-croisade-testost%C3%A9rone-pete-hegseth-extr%C3%AAme-droite-maga-masculinisme) did not. Its French-language piece was titled "L'homme 'High-T' ou la nouvelle croisade testostéronée de Pete Hegseth et de l'extrême droite" — framing the policy as ideological crusade rather than military readiness initiative. The Asia Times led with "Hegseth mocked" without attribution. [CBC World](https://www.cbc.ca/news/world/hegseth-us-troops-testosterone-9.7271460?cmp=rss) identified Hegseth under his official title — "U.S. Secretary of War" — a designation that carries editorial weight of its own. Across 14 articles from 13 outlets in 10 countries, the consensus was skepticism or ridicule, in terms that ranged from dry to pointed.

The UK's coverage was the most medically substantive and the most isolated from the rest of the corpus, with a mean similarity of 0.586 — the lowest bucket in the cluster. [The Independent](https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-politics/hegseth-military-testosterone-screening-expert-opinion-b3016473.html) led with medical expert critique: "The Pentagon's plans to screen military members for testosterone lacks scientific backing, medical experts warn." The reporting explained that hormone levels naturally fluctuate throughout the day based on stress, sleep, and physical exertion, and that "single-point screening" produces unreliable results. That specific medical argument — the methodology is unsound — appeared nowhere else in the corpus. UK press turned the story into a science critique; other outlets turned it into a political or cultural story.

[The Atlantic](https://www.theatlantic.com/health/2026/07/hegseth-testosterone-testing-military/687929/?utm_source=feed) assessed the policy's practical implications: "a questionable plan to monitor the hormone levels of every service member over 30." [Globe and Mail](https://www.theglobeandmail.com/world/article-pete-hegseth-testosterone-testing-us-military-members/) noted the Pentagon would "offer testosterone replacement therapy to keep troops 'on the leading edge of lethality'" — quoting the Pentagon's own framing directly, which rendered the claim self-undermining in print.

[Mother Jones](https://www.motherjones.com/politics/2026/07/the-manosphere-and-christian-nationalist-roots-of-hegseths-low-t-fixation/) provided the ideological genealogy no international outlet attempted: tracing the policy's roots to manosphere and Christian nationalist discourse, framing Hegseth's "Low-T fixation" as the institutional expression of an ideological movement rather than a readiness initiative. Indian press — [The Hindu](https://www.thehindu.com/sci-tech/health/what-is-testosterone-screening-and-why-is-the-us-military-introducing-it-explained/article71233072.ece) — explained the policy's mechanics without editorialising: what testosterone screening involves, what the normal range is, what therapy would consist of. Brazilian outlet [Folha de São Paulo](https://redir.folha.com.br/redir/online/mundo/rss091/*https://www1.folha.uol.com.br/mundo/2026/07/secretario-de-trump-anuncia-teste-obrigatorio-de-testosterona-para-militares-incluindo-mulheres.shtml) noted, factually but pointedly, that the mandatory screening includes women.

China's state media — Xinhua, Global Times — did not appear in this cluster despite a story offering ready material for commentary on U.S. military readiness and institutional dysfunction. Chinese state outlets characteristically weigh geopolitical cost before engaging American political spectacle; the proximity of the September 2026 U.S.-China summit likely raised the cost above the benefit of amplification. The silence is a strategic editorial decision, not an oversight.

The pairwise similarity between French and UK coverage was 0.456 — the lowest in the corpus — reflecting how differently two Western European press traditions treated the same announcement. France framed it as ideological; the UK framed it as scientifically unsound. Both were critical, but they were critical in ways that would not easily translate into each other's editorial vocabulary. The policy generated international attention without generating international consensus on what, exactly, was wrong with it.
